narration: Ever see a face in your toast? You're not alone.

narration: This phenomenon is called pareidolia. Our brains are hardwired to recognize faces.
visual_description: Context visual: This phenomenon is called pareidolia. Our brains are hardwired to recognize faces.
ai_image_prompt: Context visual: This phenomenon is called pareidolia. Our brains are hardwired to recognize faces.
ai_video_prompt: Cinematic motion: Context visual: This phenomenon is called pareidolia. Our brains are hardwired to recognize faces.

narration: It's a survival mechanism! Spotting faces helps us connect socially and identify threats.
visual_description: Escalation visual: It's a survival mechanism! Spotting faces helps us connect socially and identify threats.
ai_image_prompt: Escalation visual: It's a survival mechanism! Spotting faces helps us connect socially and identify threats.
ai_video_prompt: Cinematic motion: Escalation visual: It's a survival mechanism! Spotting faces helps us connect socially and identify threats.

narration: Research in cognitive psychology shows this happens across cultures and ages.
visual_description: Evidence visual: Research in cognitive psychology shows this happens across cultures and ages.
ai_image_prompt: Evidence visual: Research in cognitive psychology shows this happens across cultures and ages.
ai_video_prompt: Cinematic motion: Evidence visual: Research in cognitive psychology shows this happens across cultures and ages.

narration: So next time you see a face in your toast, remember: it’s your brain’s way of connecting the dots.
